old_claim.note: Instrument Calibration Standards provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: The 1478 engraved maps are documented through intaglio cutting and punched lettering; no calibrated-instrument institution is required for that print process.

source_shape.source_locator: Pages 3 and 10 explain copperplate intaglio map printing and identify the 1478 Rome Ptolemy's Geography as using punched lettering throughout its engraved maps.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The chapter identifies copperplate intaglio technique and punched lettering throughout the 1478 Rome Ptolemy maps.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Engraving reproduces existing cartographic content on a plate; calibration may improve later surveys but is not part of the documented plate-making operation.

ontology_before: precision_map_engraving inherited directly from instrument_calibration_standards as an origin or component dependency.
ontology_after: precision_map_engraving no longer inherits from instrument_calibration_standards; the source-pinned scope is represented by its remaining prerequisites.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: precision_map_engraving no longer has a direct prerequisite edge from instrument_calibration_standards.
would_reject_if: Direct historical evidence showed that instrument_calibration_standards was an operational prerequisite for the source-pinned precision_map_engraving milestone rather than later context or a parallel practice.
short_reason: Instrument calibration is not documented as a prerequisite for the 1478 engraving method.
